title: CPQ Exercises — Integration & Architecture
description: Hands-on exercises covering REST API exploration, data loading via API, integration architecture design, tenant management, and release readiness.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Explore the CPQ REST API with Postman
sections.content:
Objective

Get hands-on with the SAP CPQ REST API by making authenticated calls to read products, quotes, and pricebook data. This builds the foundation for any integration work.

Prerequisites

Install Postman (or any REST client). Have access to a CPQ sandbox tenant with admin credentials or an API-enabled service account.

Steps

Part A — Authentication

  1. Create a new Postman collection named "CPQ API Exercises".
  2. Set up Basic Authentication at the collection level using your CPQ username and password (Base64 encoded in the Authorization header).
  3. Make a simple GET request to the CPQ API base URL (e.g. https://your-tenant.cpq.cloud.sap/api/) to verify connectivity. You should receive a response (possibly a list of available endpoints or a welcome message).

Part B — Read Products

  1. Make a GET request to the products endpoint to retrieve a list of products.
  2. Filter by a specific product name or part number you created in Phase 1/2 exercises.
  3. Retrieve the full detail of a configurable product — note the attribute structure in the response (attributes, values, rules).
  4. Save the response and study the JSON structure: product ID, name, part number, display type, attributes array, attribute values.

Part C — Read & Create Quotes

  1. Make a GET request to retrieve existing quotes.
  2. Study the quote JSON structure: header fields, line items, custom fields, status, customer data.
  3. Make a POST request to create a new quote programmatically. Set the minimum required fields (customer, market). Note the returned quote ID.
  4. Make a subsequent POST to add a product as a line item to the quote you just created.

Part D — Pricebook Data

  1. Make a GET request to retrieve pricebook entries for a specific market and pricebook.
  2. Study the structure: part number, price, cost, price code, validity dates.

Validation

You should have a working Postman collection with at least 6 requests: authentication test, product list, product detail, quote list, quote creation, and pricebook read. Save this collection — you'll use it for future integration work. Document the endpoint URLs and key response field names in a reference sheet.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Bulk Product Load via API
sections.content:
Objective

Use the CPQ API to load a batch of products with their attributes and attribute-to-product links — demonstrating the API's key advantage over Excel import.

Scenario

You need to load 5 simple products and 1 configurable product with 2 attributes into CPQ, including the attribute-to-product links that Excel cannot create.

Steps

Part A — Prepare the Data

  1. Create a JSON file (or Postman collection runner data file) with the following products:
    • 5 simple products: API_Keyboard_01 through API_Keyboard_05, each with Part Number, Description, Category, and Base Price.
    • 1 configurable product: API_Workstation with Part Number WS001.
  2. Prepare the attribute data: 2 attributes (API_Screen_Size: 24", 27", 32" and API_Memory: 16GB, 32GB, 64GB).

Part B — Execute the API Load

  1. Using Postman (or a script in Python/JavaScript), make API calls in the correct top-down order:
    • Step 1: Create the category (if it doesn't exist).
    • Step 2: Create the 5 simple products.
    • Step 3: Create the 2 attributes with their values.
    • Step 4: Create the configurable product.
    • Step 5: Assign attributes to the configurable product with visibility settings — this is the step Excel cannot do.
  2. For each API call, log the request, response status, and returned ID.

Part C — Verify in the UI

  1. Log into the CPQ admin UI.
  2. Navigate to Products and find your API-loaded products.
  3. Open the configurable product and verify the attributes are assigned with the correct values visible.
  4. Test the configuration in Layout → Configuration Preview.

Validation

The configurable product should be fully functional — attributes assigned, values visible, configuration preview working. This proves that the API can achieve what Excel import cannot: creating the full product-attribute link structure programmatically. Document any errors encountered and how you resolved them (case sensitivity, missing parent objects, etc.).

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Design an Integration Architecture
sections.content:
Objective

Design a complete integration architecture for a hypothetical client landscape. This is a paper exercise (no sandbox required) that develops the solution design thinking needed for real projects and the certification exam.

Scenario

A mid-size manufacturing company has the following landscape:

  • SAP S/4HANA — ERP with 5,000 materials, pricing conditions (PR00, condition records), and customer master data.
  • SAP Sales Cloud (C4C) — CRM used by 50 sales reps for opportunity management.
  • SAP CPQ — being implemented for configurable product quoting.
  • DocuSign — for customer e-signatures on quotes.

Design Tasks

Task 1 — Data Flow Diagram

Draw a diagram showing the data flows between all four systems. For each flow, indicate: the data domain (products, prices, customers, quotes, orders, signatures), the direction (inbound/outbound/bidirectional), and the integration pattern (real-time API, batch/file, event-driven).

Task 2 — Data Ownership Matrix

Create a table defining which system is the master for each data domain: material/product master, pricing, customer master, opportunities, quotes, orders, and signed documents. For shared data, specify the direction of sync.

Task 3 — Integration Technology Choices

For each data flow, recommend the technology: SAP Integration Suite (CPI) with pre-built iFlows, direct REST API calls from CPQ scripts, IronPython outbound HTTP for e-signature, or another approach. Justify each choice based on the four integration factors (volume, latency, direction, transformation complexity).

Task 4 — Error Handling Strategy

For the two most critical integration points (order handoff to S/4HANA and e-signature callback from DocuSign), define: what happens on failure, retry logic, notification to users, and manual intervention process.

Validation

Review your design against the integration patterns covered in the training material. Check: is every data domain accounted for? Is every integration point using an appropriate technology? Is real-time only used where the business requires it? Are error scenarios handled? Have a colleague or mentor review the design and challenge your decisions.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Tenant Management & Release Readiness
sections.content:
Objective

Practise the operational discipline of managing CPQ tenants, deployment packages, application parameters, and release readiness — skills that are critical for production operations.

Steps

Part A — Document the Tenant Landscape

  1. Log into each available CPQ tenant (Dev, Test, Production — or whichever you have access to).
  2. For each tenant, navigate to Setup → Home and record: tenant URL, CPQ version number, and environment purpose.
  3. Create a tenant inventory document listing all tenants, their version, purpose, and who has access.

Part B — Application Parameter Audit

  1. In your development tenant, navigate to Setup → General → Application Parameters.
  2. Review the following parameters and document their current values:
    • Enable Promotions
    • Create Empty Quote First
    • Shopping Cart and Quotes settings
    • Any parameters related to recurring pricing
  3. Compare parameter values across tenants (if you have access to more than one). Document any discrepancies — these are potential bugs when configuration works in Dev but not in Production because a parameter wasn't replicated.

Part C — Deployment Package Practice

  1. Create a small deployment package from your Dev tenant: select one configurable product with its attributes and rules.
  2. Export the package.
  3. If you have a Test tenant, import the package there. Verify the product, attributes, and rules are correctly transferred.
  4. Document the process: what was included, any warnings during import, and verification steps.

Part D — Release Notes Review

  1. Navigate to Setup → Home and find the link to the latest CPQ release notes.
  2. Read the release notes for the current version. Identify:
    • 3 new features that could be useful for your exercises or a client project.
    • Any breaking changes or deprecated features that would require action.
    • Any changes to APIs that would affect the Postman collection from Exercise 1.
  3. Write a one-paragraph summary of the release's impact on a hypothetical production deployment.

Validation

You should have four deliverables: a tenant inventory document, an application parameter audit with cross-tenant comparison, deployment package documentation, and a release notes impact summary. These are exactly the artifacts a CPQ consultant produces during real project operations.
